title: Compassion Benchmark Daily Briefing — June 25, 2026
headline: 42 states subpoenaed OpenAI — none of the AI and health legal actions move scores until courts decide.
summary: June 25 is a confirmation-dominant cycle: 20 assessments, zero score changes, and four false-alarm signals stopped before they could distort the record. The cycle's defining theme is a wave of legal and regulatory actions against AI and health companies — none of which has yet produced a concluded proceeding. OpenAI faces a 42-state attorney general subpoena, the broadest consumer-protection action ever filed against a frontier AI company. Florida is also investigating OpenAI criminally. UnitedHealth Group faces an expanded Department of Justice (DOJ) criminal and antitrust probe into Medicare billing. Humana faces a DOJ False Claims Act complaint and a class-action lawsuit over AI-driven claim denials. Apple faces a labor-board charge after closing its first unionized store. xAI, the company behind Grok, received an adverse privacy ruling in Canada and faces the first AI-safety whistleblower-retaliation lawsuit. The benchmark waits for court outcomes, not headlines — so all scores hold. Four screening checks stopped false signals: a stale Anthropic score figure in the scan data (59.1, not 60.0), a Microsoft evidence item already counted on June 23, a Nigeria item already counted on June 19, and a Mali score question correctly routed as a calibration review for the second cycle running. Meanwhile, Sudan's famine is now officially declared in two cities (El-Fasher and Kadugli) while the Rapid Support Forces' drone campaign continues. Iran executed 31 people in four days — one every three hours. The Democratic Republic of the Congo's (DRC) Ebola outbreak reached 1,094 cases and 277 deaths with the July natural peak now weeks away. Five rotation-backfill countries — Guyana, Suriname, Belize, Solomon Islands — plus 1X Technologies confirmed at their starting-point scores of 35.9 and 50.0 respectively (low confidence; no recent evidence found).

topSignals.title: AI and Health Companies Face a Wave of Legal Actions — Scores Hold Until Courts Decide
topSignals.whyItMatters: Five companies — OpenAI, UnitedHealth, Humana, Apple, and xAI — now face simultaneous active legal proceedings. No score moves until courts or regulators reach a conclusion. This is the most concentrated legal pressure on AI and health companies in the benchmark's history.
topSignals.description: A cluster of serious legal actions arrived this cycle, none of which moves a score yet. OpenAI faces a 42-state attorney general subpoena — led by New York's Letitia James — targeting data practices, handling of minors, model behavior, and internal safety policies. That is the broadest consumer-protection action ever filed against a frontier AI company. Florida's attorney general is separately running a criminal investigation tied to ChatGPT and the Florida State University shooting. UnitedHealth Group's DOJ probe expanded: it now covers both criminal Medicare Advantage overbilling and antitrust questions about the Optum relationship. Humana has an active DOJ False Claims Act complaint (kickbacks and disability discrimination) and a class-action lawsuit (Barrows v. Humana) over its nH Predict AI algorithm denying nursing-home claims for an 86-year-old plaintiff. Apple closed its first-ever unionized store — Towson, Maryland — and the machinists' union filed an unfair-labor-practice charge, saying union workers were not offered the same relocation terms as workers at non-union stores. xAI received an adverse ruling from Canada's privacy regulator (the Office of the Privacy Commissioner): Grok's image tool generated more than 6,000 non-consensual sexualized deepfakes per hour, targeting women and children. A separate whistleblower lawsuit says xAI fired an engineer before he could present safety concerns. The benchmark's rule: investigations, complaints, and early-stage lawsuits are not the same as a finding of guilt. They are warning signs. Scores move when a proceeding concludes — not when it opens.

evidence.quote: OpenAI hit with sweeping probe from massive coalition of 42 US state attorneys general
evidence.claim: 42 state AGs, led by NY AG Letitia James, sent OpenAI a subpoena on June 13 covering ads, data practices, handling of minors and seniors, model sycophancy, and internal safety policies — the broadest consumer-protection action ever against a frontier AI company.

evidence.quote: xAI fired an engineer who raised alarms about Grok safety, new lawsuit claims
evidence.claim: The Kim v. xAI whistleblower-retaliation lawsuit, filed in Santa Clara Superior Court, is the first case of its kind: an AI safety engineer alleging he was fired before he could present safety concerns to leadership.

evidence.quote: OPC investigation finds X Corp. violated PIPEDA
evidence.claim: Canada's Office of the Privacy Commissioner found that X Corp./xAI violated Canada's privacy law; Grok's image tool generated 6,000-plus non-consensual sexualized deepfakes per hour; a privacy-impact assessment was not completed until March 2026, months after the July 2025 launch.
evidence.source: Office of the Privacy Commissioner of Canada

topSignals.title: Sudan 0 of 100: Famine Now Declared in El-Fasher and Kadugli — Drone Campaign Continues
topSignals.whyItMatters: The UN has officially declared famine in two Sudanese cities while a military drone campaign keeps hospitals offline and 500,000 civilians trapped. Sudan has the lowest possible score. The evidence is accumulating, not retreating.
topSignals.description: Sudan holds at zero out of 100 — the lowest possible score. Two developments this cycle add to the record. First, famine has been officially declared in El-Fasher (North Darfur) and Kadugli (South Kordofan) — the highest level of food-crisis designation. Second, the Rapid Support Forces' (RSF) drone campaign against El Obeid has passed its 14th consecutive day. Hospitals in El Obeid are offline. About 500,000 civilians remain in the city. A UN expert said the city is 'weeks from catastrophe.' A 29-country coalition at the UN Human Rights Council issued a coordinated atrocity warning on June 18. The US State Department's formal atrocity-threat statement, issued June 22, remains in effect. The RSF's documented playbook — drone strikes on infrastructure, then direct assault on residential areas — is what happened in El Fasher in May 2025, killing roughly 6,000 civilians in three days. The evidence base for Sudan's floor designation is deepening every cycle.

evidence.quote: Sudan city 'weeks from catastrophe,' UN official says
evidence.claim: A UN official stated on June 24 that El Obeid is weeks from catastrophe after ten-plus days of RSF drone strikes; hospitals offline; 500,000 civilians trapped.

evidence.quote: Dozens of countries warn of atrocities amid escalation in Sudan's El Obeid
evidence.claim: A 29-country coalition at the UN Human Rights Council issued a coordinated warning on June 18 of imminent atrocities against El Obeid's civilian population.

topSignals.title: Iran 2.5 of 100: 31 Executions in Four Days — One Every Three Hours
topSignals.whyItMatters: Iran executed 31 people in four days in June — the fastest documented political-execution rate in at least 37 years. This is not a trend reversal. It is an acceleration of a pattern already priced into Iran's near-floor score.
topSignals.description: Iran holds at 2.5 out of 100. Between June 13 and 16, Iran executed 31 prisoners — one roughly every three hours. Year-to-date, Iran has carried out at least 784 executions in 2026. That is the highest rate in 37 years. Forty-five or more of those were political executions. Seventy-eight protesters are at risk of expedited execution; 18 have already been executed for participating in protests. Hunger strikes against the death penalty have been running for 120 or more consecutive weeks across 56 prisons. The UN has documented all of this. Iran's score does not fall to the absolute floor (zero) because some state functions remain — courts issue rulings, some appeals occur. But the evidence base is among the most severe in the world on the dimension measuring whether a state respects basic protections (Boundaries).

evidence.quote: Iranian regime's execution spree: 31 prisoners killed in four days, one every three hours
evidence.claim: Iran's National Council of Resistance documented 31 executions in four days (June 13-16 2026), the fastest execution rate in recent Iranian history, as part of a year-to-date total of 784-plus executions — a 37-year high.

evidence.quote: As executions surge in Iran, prisoners risk their lives to protest the state's killings
evidence.claim: Iran Human Rights documents 784-plus executions year-to-date 2026 — the highest rate in 37 years — plus 125 consecutive weeks of hunger strikes across 56 prisons against the practice.

topSignals.title: DRC 2.3 of 100: Ebola Reaches 1,094 Cases and 277 Deaths — July Peak Now Weeks Away
topSignals.whyItMatters: The DRC's Ebola outbreak is the second-largest ever recorded and is spreading faster than any prior outbreak. There is no approved vaccine. The July natural peak is weeks away. A score change could follow if armed groups block WHO response teams.
topSignals.description: The Democratic Republic of the Congo (DRC) holds at 2.3 out of 100. As of June 22, the Bundibugyo-strain Ebola outbreak had 1,094 confirmed cases and 277 deaths. It reached 250 deaths in 37 days — compared with 78 days in the 2014 West Africa outbreak, which killed more than 11,000 people. This is now the second-largest Ebola outbreak on record and the fastest-growing one. No approved vaccine exists for this strain. Contact tracing is running at 45 percent — far below the 90 percent needed to contain an outbreak. The World Health Organization (WHO) declared a Public Health Emergency of International Concern (PHEIC) in May 2026. The DRC government did not cause this outbreak. The near-floor score reflects collapsed state capacity, not deliberate state harm. The conversion trigger for a lower score: if the M23 armed group (backed by Rwanda) blocks WHO burial teams or response personnel, the obstruction would be attributed to Rwanda, which is already scored separately for its military conduct in eastern DRC.

evidence.quote: Ebola outbreak in DRC and Uganda — European Centre for Disease Prevention and Control
evidence.claim: ECDC documents the DRC Ebola outbreak as the second-largest on record and the fastest-growing Bundibugyo-strain event ever; as of late June 2026, the outbreak had spread across the border into Uganda.

evidence.quote: UN News: DRC Ebola update June 2026
evidence.claim: UN News reports the DRC Ebola outbreak reached 1,094 cases and 277 deaths by June 22, with contact tracing at 45 percent versus the 90 percent needed; WHO PHEIC declared May 16.

topSignals.title: Israel 0 of 100: 72,996 or More Palestinians Killed — Amnesty Finds Lebanon Evacuation Orders Are a War Crime
topSignals.whyItMatters: Israel's floor score is reinforced this cycle with a new category of evidence: Amnesty International found that Israel's mass evacuation orders in Lebanon constitute the war crime of unlawful transfer. The Gaza death toll exceeds 72,996. No lower score is possible.
topSignals.description: Israel holds at zero out of 100. Two evidence streams add to the record this cycle. In Gaza, 72,996 or more Palestinians have been killed since October 2023. Aid entry has dropped to about 145 trucks per day — against 600 agreed. UNRWA, the main UN aid agency for Palestinians, has been blocked from direct entry since January 2025. In Lebanon, Amnesty International published a June 2026 report finding that Israel 'radically expanded' its mass evacuation orders and committed the war crime of unlawful transfer — forcible displacement of civilians. The displacement orders covered 29 towns in southern Lebanon. Amnesty says the orders were broad and indiscriminate. The harm in Lebanon counts against Israel, not Lebanon. Lebanon's score is based on Lebanon's own conduct toward its people.

evidence.quote: Lebanon: Israel radically expands use of unlawful mass evacuation orders and commits war crime of unlawful transfer
evidence.claim: Amnesty International's June 2026 report finds that Israel's mass evacuation orders covering 29 towns in southern Lebanon constitute the war crime of unlawful transfer under international humanitarian law.

evidence.quote: UNRWA Situation Report 226 — Humanitarian Crisis in Gaza Strip and Occupied West Bank
evidence.claim: UNRWA Sit-Rep 226 documents 72,996-plus Palestinian deaths, 392 UNRWA staff killed, UNRWA blocked since January 2025, and aid at 145 trucks per day against 600 agreed.

topSignals.title: Anthropic 59.1 of 100: US Export-Control Order Attributed to the Government, Not Anthropic
topSignals.whyItMatters: The US government forced two Anthropic AI models offline. The benchmark scores who took the action. Anthropic complied while objecting publicly. Its score stays at 59.1 — just 0.9 points below the next higher band. Tomorrow's Congressional deadline is the next observable event.
topSignals.description: Anthropic holds at 59.1 out of 100 — among the highest of any AI company in the benchmark. The US Commerce Department's export-control order forced global suspension of two Anthropic models (Claude Fable 5 and Mythos 5) — the first time export controls have ever been applied to a commercially deployed AI model. The benchmark's rule: we score the entity that took the action. The US government took this action. Anthropic is scored on its own response. Anthropic complied with the order while publicly stating it disagreed: the company said a 'narrow jailbreak does not justify global withdrawal.' That is consistent with its existing record of transparent, principled engagement under pressure. Four members of Congress — two from each party — sent a letter to Commerce Secretary Lutnick on June 18 demanding an explanation by June 26. That deadline is tomorrow. Anthropic's score sits 0.9 points below the boundary between its current band (Functional) and the next higher band (Established). The government's response — or non-response — is the next scheduled evidence point.

evidence.quote: Anthropic disables Fable 5 and Mythos 5 after US export controls
evidence.claim: The US Commerce/BIS export-control order forced global suspension of Claude Fable 5 and Mythos 5 — the first-ever export control applied to a commercially deployed AI model; attributed to the US government, not Anthropic.

evidence.quote: Bipartisan members of Congress seek transparency on frontier AI export controls
evidence.claim: Four bipartisan members of Congress sent Commerce a letter on June 18 demanding a written explanation of the Fable 5 and Mythos 5 export-control order by June 26.

topSignals.title: Somalia 4.7 of 100: Famine Projected in Buur Hakaba by End of June — WFP Reaching One in Ten People
topSignals.whyItMatters: Somalia is within weeks of a formal famine declaration in a specific district. That declaration would be a score-change trigger. The World Food Programme is currently reaching only one in ten people who need food.
topSignals.description: Somalia holds at 4.7 out of 100 — a near-floor designation. A famine declaration in Buur Hakaba district is projected by the end of June 2026. The credible famine risk extends to Bay, Bakool, and Gedo regions through September. About six million Somalis — one in three people — face life-threatening food insecurity. More than 1.9 million children are acutely malnourished. The World Food Programme is reaching only one in ten people who need food — a sharp drop from last year. Al-Shabaab is taxing water, food, and livestock deliveries, and destroying some food shipments outright. Food prices have risen 70 percent in some areas; fuel prices are up 150 percent. If a formal famine declaration is made for Buur Hakaba, that constitutes a scored change trigger for Somalia — a famine declaration is treated as a concluded, authoritative finding, not a forecast.

evidence.quote: Somalia barrels toward possible famine amid aid cuts
evidence.claim: Crisis Group analysis documents WFP reaching only 1-in-10 Somalis needing food, projected famine in Buur Hakaba by end of June, and credible risk through September in Bay, Bakool, and Gedo.

evidence.quote: WFP warns Somalia's humanitarian crisis worsening — 6.5 million people facing high levels of hunger
evidence.claim: WFP reports 6.5 million Somalis facing high levels of hunger, 1.9 million children acutely malnourished, and food prices up 70 percent in some areas amid Al-Shabaab obstruction.

topSignals.title: xAI/Grok 0 of 100: Canada Rules Privacy Law Violated — Engineer Fired for Raising Safety Concerns
topSignals.whyItMatters: xAI faces two concurrent legal findings: a government privacy regulator found the company violated Canadian law, and an engineer says he was fired for raising safety alarms. Both are now in the formal record. The floor score cannot go lower.
topSignals.description: xAI holds at zero out of 100 — the lowest possible score. This cycle produced two concurrent active proceedings. Canada's Office of the Privacy Commissioner issued an adverse ruling on June 11: it found X Corp./xAI violated Canada's privacy law (PIPEDA). The investigation found Grok's image tool generated more than 6,000 non-consensual sexualized deepfakes per hour targeting women and children. xAI did not complete a privacy-impact assessment until March 2026 — months after the July 2025 launch. Separately, a whistleblower lawsuit filed in Santa Clara Superior Court (Kim v. xAI) says xAI fired an engineer before he could present safety concerns to leadership. That is the first known AI-safety whistleblower-retaliation case on record. The floor-level score captures the severity of harms confirmed across multiple evidence streams. Both proceedings are pre-adjudication — the score would stay at zero regardless, because it reflects the confirmed pattern, and zero is already the minimum.

evidence.quote: OPC investigation finds X Corp. violated PIPEDA in connection with Grok AI tool
evidence.claim: Canada's OPC found X Corp./xAI violated PIPEDA; Grok generated 6,000-plus non-consensual sexualized deepfakes per hour; no privacy-impact assessment until March 2026, months post-launch.
evidence.source: Office of the Privacy Commissioner of Canada

topSignals.title: Mali 12.5 of 100: Three Opposition Figures Disappeared in Bamako in May — Calibration Review Continues
topSignals.whyItMatters: Three people who criticized Mali's military government disappeared in Bamako in May. UN investigators already say Malian forces and Wagner have committed enforced disappearances that may constitute crimes against humanity. Mali's score is under review against neighboring Burkina Faso's lower score of 6.3.
topSignals.description: Mali holds at 12.5 out of 100 — a starting-point estimate, not a score based on a full evidence review. New within-window evidence: three opposition figures and critics of Mali's military government disappeared in Bamako between May 8 and 11. UN Special Procedures investigators documented earlier this year that Malian government forces and the Wagner mercenary group have carried out enforced disappearances following a 'systematic pattern' that 'may constitute crimes against humanity.' That UN characterization is serious but not yet formally adjudicated — the benchmark does not move scores on pre-adjudication findings. The score question is not just about Mali alone: neighboring Burkina Faso was fully reassessed in June 2026 on comparable UN-documented state-perpetration conduct and now scores 6.3. Mali's 12.5 is higher than Burkina Faso's 6.3, despite comparable documented conduct. Whether Mali's score is correctly calibrated relative to its Sahel neighbors is the subject of a coordinator-level calibration review — now in its second cycle. No score change is made this cycle.

evidence.quote: Mali: UN experts demand release of forcibly disappeared persons
evidence.claim: UN Special Procedures documented that enforced disappearances by Malian forces and Wagner follow a systematic pattern; their frequency, organised nature, and methods 'may constitute crimes against humanity.'

topSignals.title: UnitedHealth Group 10.2 of 100: DOJ Criminal and Antitrust Probe Expanded — Score Holds Under Pre-Adjudication Rule
topSignals.whyItMatters: UnitedHealth faces simultaneous criminal and antitrust investigations by the DOJ. Its score of 10.2 out of 100 already reflects a documented pattern of harm. The score does not move further until investigators reach a conclusion.
topSignals.description: UnitedHealth Group holds at 10.2 out of 100 — near the bottom of the Fortune 500 index. The DOJ's investigation expanded this cycle. It now covers Medicare Advantage overbilling — specifically, inflated diagnoses entered through in-home nurse assessments — plus Optum Rx billing practices, physician reimbursement, and an antitrust question about the Optum/insurance relationship. UnitedHealth disclosed the probe in an SEC filing and launched a third-party billing review. The broader backdrop: on June 23, the DOJ charged 455 defendants in a nationwide healthcare-fraud sweep alleging $6.5 billion in fraud across the industry. That sweep is sector context — it is not a UnitedHealth-specific finding. The existing score of 10.2 already reflects the documented pattern of harm to Medicare beneficiaries and denial of coverage. The pre-adjudication rule: a DOJ investigation that has not yet produced a criminal conviction, civil settlement, or consent decree does not automatically move the score further. The score moves when the proceeding concludes.

evidence.quote: UnitedHealth acknowledges federal probe of Medicare Advantage practices
evidence.claim: UnitedHealth disclosed in an SEC filing that the DOJ is conducting criminal and civil investigations into Medicare Advantage overbilling via in-home nurse assessments and into Optum Rx billing practices.

boundaryWatch.trigger: US Congress June 26 deadline for Commerce to explain the Fable 5 and Mythos 5 export-control order. The government's written response — or non-response — is the next scheduled evidence point.
boundaryWatch.note: Anthropic sits 0.9 points below the Functional-to-Established boundary at 60.0. Five consecutive cycles in this range. The export-control order is attributed to the US government; Anthropic's transparent, principled response is consistent with its existing score. The June 26 Commerce deadline is the next observable event.

boundaryWatch.trigger: NLRB review of the unfair-labor-practice charge filed after Apple closed its Towson, Maryland store — its first unionized store. An NLRB complaint or ruling is the conversion trigger.
boundaryWatch.note: Apple sits 0.6 points below the Functional-to-Established boundary at 60.0. The machinists' union charge alleges union workers received different terms than non-union workers at simultaneously closing stores. The charge has not yet resulted in an NLRB complaint. First boundary-watch cycle.

boundaryWatch.trigger: July natural Ebola peak window; M23 territory overlap with WHO response corridors in North Kivu is the floor-crossing trigger if obstruction of the health response is documented.
boundaryWatch.note: DRC at 2.3 Critical through cycle 12 of the Ebola emergency. The outbreak reached 1,094 cases and 277 deaths in 37 days — the fastest case-fatality progression on record. Contact tracing at 45 percent. The July peak is weeks away.

boundaryWatch.trigger: Famine declaration in Buur Hakaba district projected by end of June 2026. A formal IPC Phase 5 famine declaration is a scored change trigger.
boundaryWatch.note: Somalia at 4.7 Critical, 4.7 points above the absolute floor. WFP reaching one in ten people. Famine projected in Buur Hakaba by end of June; credible risk in Bay, Bakool, Gedo through September. A formal declaration is the conversion event.

boundaryWatch.trigger: Coordinator-level Sahel-band calibration review. Burkina Faso (6.3) was reassessed in June on comparable UN-documented state-perpetration conduct. Mali's seed score of 12.5 is above the likely calibrated range.
boundaryWatch.note: Mali at 12.5 Critical on a starting-point seed score. Three Bamako opposition figures disappeared in May — adding to the documented pattern. The Sahel-band calibration review covers Mali, Niger, and potentially Chad. No timeline set.

boundaryWatch.trigger: DOJ False Claims Act complaint (kickbacks and disability discrimination) and Barrows v. Humana class action (nH Predict AI claim denials). Either concluded finding confirms the Developing-band designation.
boundaryWatch.note: Humana at 35.2 Developing, 4.8 points below the Functional boundary. Fourth cycle post-downgrade. No new adjudicated evidence this cycle. Neither the DOJ complaint nor the Barrows class action has a fixed hearing date.

boundaryWatch.trigger: Bengali Muslim and Rohingya expulsions documented in June 2026; detect-delete-deport digital surveillance continuing. Eleventh consecutive monitoring cycle at 0.6 points above the Critical floor.
boundaryWatch.note: India at 15.6 Critical sits 0.6 points above the Critical floor through an eleventh consecutive monitoring cycle.

emergingRisks.risk: Sudan's RSF drone campaign against El Obeid has continued for 14 or more days. Famine is now officially declared in El-Fasher and Kadugli — the same areas the RSF is attacking. The El Fasher sequence (drone strikes then direct assault killing roughly 6,000 civilians in three days) is the documented pattern the UN is warning against.
emergingRisks.detail: The US State Department's formal atrocity-threat warning and the 29-country UNHRC coalition warning have not changed RSF posture. El Obeid has about 500,000 civilians. A UN expert said the city is weeks from catastrophe. Famine declared in El-Fasher and Kadugli creates a compound humanitarian crisis in the same geographic area.

emergingRisks.risk: The DRC Ebola outbreak is approaching its July natural peak with 1,094 cases and 277 deaths, no approved vaccine, and contact tracing running at 45 percent — far below the 90 percent needed to stop transmission.
emergingRisks.detail: The outbreak has spread across the border into Uganda. More than 70 health workers are infected, degrading response capacity. M23-controlled territory overlaps with WHO response corridors in North Kivu. If M23 blocks burial teams, Rwanda's existing perpetrator attribution extends to a secondary obstruction pathway.

emergingRisks.risk: Somalia is within weeks of a formal famine declaration in Buur Hakaba district. A formal IPC Phase 5 famine declaration would be a scored change trigger — the closest time-bounded event to a score change in this cycle.
emergingRisks.detail: WFP is reaching one in ten people who need food. Al-Shabaab is actively obstructing food deliveries. The famine risk extends credibly through September in Bay, Bakool, and Gedo regions. The Sahel lean season peaks in August, compounding the situation.

emergingRisks.risk: The US Congress set a June 26 deadline for the Commerce Department to explain why it applied export controls to Anthropic's Fable 5 and Mythos 5 models — the first export controls ever applied to a commercially deployed AI model.
emergingRisks.detail: Four bipartisan members of Congress demanded a written explanation by June 26. Anthropic sits 0.9 points below the Functional-to-Established boundary. The government's response or non-response will be assessed in the next cycle. If the BIS order is modified or withdrawn, Anthropic's existing record of principled conduct under pressure could move its score.

emergingRisks.risk: The EU AI Act becomes fully applicable on August 2 — about 38 days away. AI companies without published compliance plans face documented exposure under the new rules. xAI and OpenAI have not published compliance roadmaps.
emergingRisks.detail: Transparency obligations for general-purpose AI models, Article 50 labeling requirements, and whistleblower protections all activate on August 2. Anthropic and DeepMind have documented compliance infrastructure. A post-applicability non-compliance finding constitutes a Tier 5 methodological finding and would move scores.

emergingRisks.risk: OpenAI faces simultaneous proceedings from 42 state attorneys general and a Florida criminal investigation — the most concentrated legal pressure on any AI company in the benchmark record. A settlement or adjudicated finding in any one of these would move OpenAI's score.
emergingRisks.detail: The 42-state subpoena covers data practices, handling of minors and seniors, model sycophancy, and internal safety policies. The Florida criminal investigation is tied to the FSU shooting. OpenAI filed a confidential IPO registration five days before the multistate subpoena was issued. Both proceedings are ongoing with no fixed hearing dates.

emergingRisks.risk: Mali's government-perpetrated enforced-disappearance pattern — with new evidence of three Bamako opposition figures disappearing in May — is now in its second cycle of calibration review against Burkina Faso (6.3).
emergingRisks.detail: Burkina Faso was reweighted in June 2026 under the active-state-perpetration framework to 6.3. Mali carries a seed score of 12.5 despite comparable UN-documented conduct. The Sahel lean season adds food-crisis pressure. A coordinator decision on the Sahel-band reweight could bring Mali's score to the 6-9 range. No timeline is set.

sectorAlerts.headline: Five AI and tech companies face simultaneous active legal proceedings — none concluded, none yet scoring.
observations: OpenAI (27.5 of 100, Developing band) faces a 42-state attorney general subpoena and a Florida criminal investigation — the broadest concurrent regulatory-legal pressure on any AI company in the benchmark record.
xAI/Grok (0 of 100, Critical band) received an adverse privacy ruling from Canada's privacy regulator and faces the first AI safety whistleblower-retaliation lawsuit. Both proceedings are active.

Anthropic (59.1 of 100, Functional band) has had two models forced offline by US export controls — government action attributed to the government, not Anthropic. The June 26 Congressional deadline is the next observable event.
Apple (59.4 of 100, Functional band) faces an NLRB unfair-labor-practice charge after closing its first unionized store. Apple sits 0.6 points below the Established band boundary.

The EU AI Act becomes fully applicable on August 2. xAI and OpenAI have not published compliance roadmaps. The gap between compliant and non-compliant labs will become more legally significant in 38 days.

sectorAlerts.headline: UnitedHealth and Humana face simultaneous DOJ probes — the DOJ's June 23 industry-wide fraud sweep adds sector pressure.
observations: UnitedHealth Group (10.2 of 100, Critical band) has an expanded DOJ criminal and antitrust probe covering Medicare Advantage overbilling, Optum Rx billing, and physician reimbursement.
Humana (35.2 of 100, Developing band) has an active DOJ False Claims Act complaint and a class-action lawsuit (Barrows v. Humana) over AI-driven nursing-home claim denials.
The June 23 DOJ healthcare fraud takedown — 455 defendants, $6.5 billion in alleged fraud — is sector-wide backdrop, not an entity-specific finding for either company.
Both scores hold under the pre-adjudication rule: neither investigation nor lawsuit has produced a concluded proceeding. Scores move when courts or regulators conclude their work.

sectorAlerts.headline: Sudan's famine declarations and Iran's execution surge add to the deepest floor cluster in the June 2026 series.
observations: Sudan (0 of 100) now has famine officially declared in El-Fasher and Kadugli while the RSF drone campaign against El Obeid continues past day 14. The UN says the city is weeks from catastrophe.

Iran (2.5 of 100) executed 31 people in four days — one every three hours — bringing the year-to-date total to 784, a 37-year high. The near-floor score reflects the severity and persistence of this pattern.
DRC (2.3 of 100) Ebola has 1,094 cases and 277 deaths with the July peak approaching. No vaccine exists for this strain.

Israel (0 of 100) has a new category of evidence this cycle: Amnesty found that mass evacuation orders in Lebanon constitute the war crime of unlawful transfer. This is own-conduct evidence by Israel.
Somalia (4.7 of 100) is the nearest entity to a score-change trigger: a formal famine declaration in Buur Hakaba would constitute an adjudicated-equivalent event.

sectorAlerts.headline: Mali calibration review enters its second cycle; 52 million people face food insecurity across the region.
observations: Mali (12.5 of 100) confirmed for the second cycle with the calibration question against Burkina Faso (6.3) still open. New evidence: three Bamako opposition figures disappeared in May.
Burkina Faso (6.3 of 100) confirmed; UN CERD Fulani extrajudicial-killing findings and ICRC drone-strike characterization continue the already-priced state-perpetration arc.
About 52.8 million people face acute food insecurity across the Sahel in the 2026 lean season — the worst in a decade.
Niger and Chad have not been assessed in this cycle. A full Sahel-band reweight would need to cover all three countries consistently with the framework applied to Burkina Faso.

sectorAlerts.headline: Four false signals stopped in one cycle — a new record; all four required different screening rules.
observations: Anthropic: the scanner reported a stale score of 60.0. The actual published score is 59.1. Additionally, the BIS export-control order is attributed to the US government, and the June 26 deadline is a future event — three separate reasons to hold.
Microsoft: the Covington investigation conclusion and governance measures were already scored on June 23 as a sub-threshold positive. Counting the same evidence again would inflate the record.
Nigeria: the IPC Phase 5 Catastrophe evidence was applied on June 19. Counting it again this week would be double-counting the same event.
Mali: a one-off downgrade was stopped because the real issue is a cross-peer calibration gap, not a single-entity event. Routed to coordinator-level review for the second consecutive cycle.

methodologyNotes.name: Who Caused the Harm — We Score the Actor, Not the Victim
methodologyNotes.description: The benchmark scores the entity that caused harm — not the one that suffered it. The US government forced Anthropic's models offline: that action is attributed to the government, not Anthropic. Israel's military operations in Lebanon count against Israel, not Lebanon. Russia's strikes on Ukraine count against Russia, not Ukraine. The same rule tells us where to look in every conflict: find who did the act, score that entity.

methodologyNotes.name: Why Legal Investigations Do Not Automatically Move Scores
methodologyNotes.description: An investigation or lawsuit opening is not the same as a finding of guilt. OpenAI faces a 42-state subpoena; UnitedHealth faces a DOJ criminal probe; Apple faces an NLRB charge; xAI faces a privacy-regulator ruling. None of these has yet concluded. The benchmark does not penalize an entity twice — once for the underlying conduct (already scored) and once for the investigation (not yet concluded). Scores move when a court, regulator, or treaty body concludes a proceeding — not when it opens.

methodologyNotes.name: How the Screening Check Stopped Four False Score Changes Tonight
methodologyNotes.description: Before any score change is proposed, evidence goes through a screening check. Tonight it stopped four false signals: (1) Anthropic's scanner figure was stale — the real score is 59.1, not 60.0. (2) Microsoft's governance evidence was already counted on June 23 — counting it again would inflate the record. (3) Nigeria's famine evidence was already counted on June 19 — same event, same cycle window. (4) Mali's new disappearance evidence looks like a single-entity event but is actually a cross-peer calibration question — it needs a region-wide review, not an individual score change.

methodologyNotes.name: Starting-Point Scores vs. Evidence-Based Scores
methodologyNotes.description: Five rotation-backfill entities confirmed tonight at seed scores of 35.9 (Guyana, Suriname, Belize, Solomon Islands) and 50.0 (1X Technologies). A seed score is a starting point — it says the benchmark has not yet completed a full evidence review for that entity. It is not a quality judgment. Seed scores for countries sit at 35.9 out of 100. Full evidence-based assessments for these entities remain outstanding.

forwardTriggers.trigger: Congressional deadline for US Commerce to explain the Fable 5 and Mythos 5 export-control order. The government's written response — or non-response — will be assessed in the next cycle.
End-of-June famine declaration window for Buur Hakaba district. A formal IPC Phase 5 famine declaration is a scored change trigger for Somalia (4.7 of 100).
Full resolution-phase reassessment due. Conversion trigger: reversal of the June 8 codified-impunity law and release of detained union leaders.
July natural Bundibugyo Ebola peak window. M23 territory overlap with WHO response corridors in North Kivu is the secondary trigger for attributing obstruction to Rwanda.
EU AI Act full applicability — general-purpose AI transparency obligations, Article 50 labeling, and whistleblower protections activate. No public compliance roadmap from xAI as of this cycle.
EU AI Act full applicability — same obligations as above apply. No public compliance roadmap from OpenAI as of this cycle.
August IPC lean-season update — IPC Phase 5 Borno trajectory and government response adequacy. The lean season peak runs through August.
RSF commencement of direct assault on El Obeid residential zones — the El Fasher pattern of drone strikes then direct assault killing 6,000 civilians in three days is the documented comparator.
Coordinator-level Sahel-band calibration review — Mali (12.5) versus reweighted peer Burkina Faso (6.3) on comparable UN-documented state-perpetration conduct. Second cycle open.
42-state attorney general proceedings and Florida criminal investigation — no fixed hearing dates; any concluded finding, settlement, or criminal charge constitutes a scored change trigger.
Extended famine window — credible risk in Bay, Bakool, and Gedo regions through September 2026. Each new IPC Phase 5 designation in Somalia is a scored change trigger.

dailyOpeningQuestion.text: If OpenAI's 42-state attorney general subpoena produces a settlement covering data practices and minor-user protections, does the Accountability dimension (currently 22.5 of 100) hold at the Developing floor, or does a consent-decree commitment to structural transparency constitute a scored Accountability improvement?
dailyOpeningQuestion.tensionFraming: Broadest-ever AI consumer-protection action opened this week — the question is what a concluded finding would mean for the score.
